stratum: Group theory & number-theoretic hardness (where security lives)
prerequisites: cyclic groups (a group generated by one element; element order; the order-q subgroup of (Z/pZ)*)
the Discrete Logarithm assumption (given g and h = g^a in a prime-order group, recovering a is infeasible)
modular arithmetic and modular inverses in Z/pZ and Z/qZ
prime-order groups (why every non-identity element is a generator; safe primes p = 2q + 1)
basic probability (a uniform distribution over a finite set leaks nothing)
assumed_background: From Ch 11's 'Reader should already know' line: group theory; elliptic-curve groups; bilinear pairings; lattices / Ring-LWE intro; number theory. This is the book's prerequisite peak.

From Ch 2's classical on-ramp: a commitment scheme as a sealed envelope, with hiding (the envelope conceals the value until opened) and binding (you cannot swap the slip after sealing) named in plain terms but NOT yet constructed.

From Ch 9-10: hiding and binding given rigorous definitions (the PCS as the polynomial special case); special soundness and the knowledge extractor met as the Schnorr/Sigma 'extract from two transcripts' move.
From Ch 11 itself: the Discrete Logarithm assumption named explicitly as the hardness bet (alongside CDH / q-SDH).
learning_objectives: State the Pedersen commitment Com(m, r) = g^m * h^r in a prime-order cyclic group with two generators g, h whose relative discrete log is unknown.

Prove PERFECT HIDING: for any fixed message m, as r ranges uniformly over Z/qZ the commitment g^m * h^r is uniformly distributed over the whole group, so C alone reveals nothing about m (an unbounded adversary still learns nothing).

Prove COMPUTATIONAL BINDING: two distinct openings (m, r) and (m', r') of the same C yield log_g(h) = (m - m')*(r' - r)^{-1} mod q, so breaking binding solves discrete log; binding holds only against bounded adversaries under the DLog assumption.
Explain the hiding/binding trade-off: Pedersen is perfectly hiding and only computationally binding; you cannot have both perfect at once for a single group element.

Compute a concrete commitment in the order-1019 subgroup of (Z/2039Z)*: with g = 9, h = 726, m = 42, r = 800 obtain C = 71, and exhibit a perfect-hiding alternate opening (m' = 100, r' = 186) giving the same C = 71.

Connect to the book's role: Pedersen is the simplest discrete-log commitment, the construction that finally BUILDS Ch 2's sealed envelope; its vector form h^r * prod g_i^{m_i} underlies IPA / Bulletproofs (the succinct opening of a vector Pedersen commitment) and additively-homomorphic vector commitments.

Distinguish Pedersen's additively-homomorphic, DLog-based, transparent commitment from a pairing/SRS-based KZG commitment (computationally binding under q-SDH, needs a trusted setup) — a Ch 11 family distinction not to be conflated.

graphify_subgraph.interpretation: The live graph places Pedersen in community 2 (Ch 11 commitment constructions / hardness worlds). Its single security-bearing edge is --assumes--> the Discrete Logarithm Assumption, which fixes the brief's central hardness prerequisite and the binding objective (breaking binding == solving DLog). The conceptually_related_to edges to the Pedersen VECTOR commitment, additively-homomorphic vector commitments, and PolyCommitPed, plus the --assumes--> edges from the Logarithmic-Size Range Proof and Bulletproof-generation MPC, confirm the book role: Pedersen is the base of the IPA/Bulletproofs vector-commitment line. The incoming reference from 'Four Families of Commitment Schemes' and the KZG paper's citation justify the brief's final objective (contrast Pedersen/DLog/transparent vs KZG/q-SDH/trusted-setup). These edges, not guesswork, set the prerequisites (cyclic groups, DLog) and the downstream connections in this brief.
